question:
Note: This question is part of a series of questions that present the same scenario. Each question in the series contains a unique solution that might meet the stated goals. Some question sets might have more than one correct solution, while others might not have a correct solution.
After you answer a question in this section, you will NOT be able to return to it. As a result, these questions will not appear in the review screen.
You have two Azure virtual networks named Vnet1 and Vnet2.
You have a Windows 10 device named Client1 that connects to Vnet1 by using a Point-to-Site (P2S) IKEv2 VPN.
You implement virtual network peering between Vnet1 and Vnet2. Vnet1 allows gateway transit. Vnet2 can use the remote gateway.
You discover that Client1 cannot communicate with Vnet2.
You need to ensure that Client1 can communicate with Vnet2.
Solution: You download and reinstall the VPN client configuration.
Does this meet the goal?
explanation:
The solution proposes downloading and reinstalling the VPN client configuration to enable Client1, connected to Vnet1 via P2S VPN, to communicate with Vnet2, which is peered with Vnet1, allowing gateway transit from Vnet1. This solution is correct.
Here's why: When a VPN client connects to a virtual network that is part of a peered network and is configured to use the remote network's gateway for transit, the client needs to have the updated routes from both networks. The initial VPN configuration that was set up might not include the routes to the peered virtual network (Vnet2 in this case).
Downloading and reinstalling the VPN client configuration ensures that the client receives the latest routing information from Azure. This new configuration will now include the necessary routes for Vnet2, enabling the client to send traffic to Vnet2 through Vnet1's gateway. Without the updated routing information, Client1 would not know how to reach Vnet2, even if peering and gateway transit are correctly configured at the virtual network level. The reinstall process effectively redistributes the client configuration package, allowing the VPN client to recognize the routes for the remote network.
Therefore, reinstalling the VPN client configuration after enabling gateway transit between the peered virtual networks resolves the issue and enables Client1 to communicate with Vnet2.
Here are some authoritative links for further research:
About VPN Gateway P2S client configuration: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn- gateway-howto-point-to-site-resource-manager-portal Configure VPN Gateway transit for virtual network peering: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn- gateway/vpn-gateway-peering-gateway-transit

question:
You have an Azure virtual network named Vnet1 that hosts an Azure firewall named FW1 and 150 virtual machines.
Vnet1 is linked to a private DNS zone named contoso.com. All the virtual machines have their name registered in the contoso.com zone.
Vnet1 connects to an on-premises datacenter by using ExpressRoute.
You need to ensure that on-premises DNS servers can resolve the names in the contoso.com zone.
Which two actions should you perform? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
options: Modify the DNS server settings of Vnet1.
For FW1, configure custom DNS server.
On the on-premises DNS servers, configure forwarders that point to the frontend IP address of FW1.
On the on-premises DNS servers, configure forwarders that point to the Azure provided DNS service at 168.63.129.16.
explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options C and D are the correct actions to take to ensure on-premises DNS servers can resolve names in the Azure private DNS zone contoso.com, and why the other options are incorrect:
Justification:
The core problem is enabling on-premises DNS servers to resolve records within the Azure private DNS zone "contoso.com." Because this zone is private, it's not directly accessible from the public internet or the on- premises network. We need a way to relay DNS queries from on-premises to Azure, and then have Azure resolve the internal names.
C. For FW1, enable DNS proxy: Enabling DNS proxy on the Azure Firewall (FW1) is crucial. This makes the firewall act as a DNS resolver on behalf of the VMs in Vnet1. Without this, the firewall won't forward DNS queries to the Azure DNS service, which has the necessary information about the "contoso.com" zone. The Azure Firewall can leverage Azure's DNS infrastructure for resolution.
D. On the on-premises DNS servers, configure forwarders that point to the frontend IP address of FW1: This is the other essential piece. You configure your on-premises DNS servers to forward queries for the "contoso.com" domain to the front-end IP address of the Azure Firewall (FW1). When the on-premises DNS server receives a query for a name in "contoso.com," it sends that query to FW1. FW1, with its DNS proxy enabled, then forwards the query to Azure DNS for resolution. Azure DNS resolves the query using the private DNS zone information.
Why other options are incorrect:
A. Modify the DNS server settings of Vnet1: While you might think about this, the virtual machines are already using the Vnet's default which points to Azure's internal DNS. Changing this might break name resolution within the Vnet. More importantly, this does not allow external on-prem resolution.
B. For FW1, configure custom DNS server: Setting a custom DNS server on the firewall would change the DNS that the firewall itself uses to resolve names. However, on-prem machines are unaware that the contoso.com domain can now be resolved by the Azure Firewall.
E. On the on-premises DNS servers, configure forwarders that point to the Azure provided DNS service at 168.63.129.16: This is incorrect for two primary reasons. First, directly using 168.63.129.16 is not supported in all scenarios, particularly when dealing with private DNS zones. The firewall acts as a secure and managed proxy to the Azure DNS service. Second, the on-prem DNS servers would need to pass through the ExpressRoute connection which they may not be authorized to do.
Authoritative Links:
Azure Firewall DNS Proxy: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/firewall/dns-proxy Azure Private DNS Zones: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/dns/private-dns-overview Hybrid DNS Resolution: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/reference- architectures/networking/hybrid-dns-infra

question:
You are planning the IP addressing for the subnets in Azure virtual networks.
Which type of resource requires IP addresses in the subnets?
explanation:
The correct answer is A, internal load balancers. Internal load balancers in Azure require IP addresses from the subnet in which they are deployed. When you create an internal load balancer, you must assign it a private IP address from the subnet. This IP address acts as the front-end IP for the load balancer and distributes traffic to the back-end pool instances.
Storage accounts (B) don't directly consume IP addresses within subnets. They have public endpoints or can be integrated with private endpoints, which then consume IP addresses. Azure Virtual Network NAT (C) is used for outbound connectivity and while it uses an IP address, it's typically a public IP or a private IP from a dedicated subnet for NAT, not IP addresses within a regular application subnet. Service endpoint policies (D) are access control mechanisms and don't themselves consume IP addresses. They determine which subnets can access specific Azure services. Therefore, only internal load balancers explicitly require IP addresses from the virtual network subnet for their functionality.
Further Reading:
Azure Load Balancer: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/ Azure Virtual Network: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/ Azure Private Endpoint: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-overview

question:
You have a hub-and-spoke topology. The topology includes multiple on-premises locations that connect to a hub virtual network in Azure via ExpressRoute circuits.
You have an Azure Application Gateway named GW1 that provides a single point of ingress from the internet.
You plan to migrate the hub-and-spoke topology to Azure Virtual WAN.
You need to identify which changes must be applied to the existing topology. The solution must ensure that you maintain a single point of ingress from the internet.
Which three changes should you include in the solution? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
options: Replace the user-defined routes used by the current topology.
Remove the existing virtual network peerings.
explanation:
The correct answer is CDE. Here's why:
C. Replace the user-defined routes used by the current topology: When transitioning to Virtual WAN, the existing UDRs that manage traffic flow within the hub-and-spoke network become irrelevant. Virtual WAN handles routing automatically through its central routing infrastructure. Therefore, you need to replace these old UDRs with configurations supported by Virtual WAN, such as route propagation and associations.
D. Create virtual network connections: Virtual WAN connects virtual networks (VNets) using virtual network connections. These connections establish the connectivity between the VNets and the Virtual WAN hub. Each spoke VNet will need a connection to the Virtual WAN hub to enable communication. This essentially replaces the peerings in the original hub-and-spoke model.
E. Remove the existing virtual network peerings: Virtual WAN inherently manages the connectivity and routing between connected VNets. Therefore, the virtual network peerings that previously connected the hub and spoke VNets become redundant and must be removed to avoid routing conflicts and ensure Virtual WAN has full control over the network topology.
Why other options are incorrect:
A. Add user-defined routes: While UDRs are still supported in VNets connected to a Virtual WAN, they are not the primary way to control routing between VNets connected to the Virtual WAN hub. Virtual WAN uses routing policies and route propagation, which simplifies routing management. Adding UDRs in this case could create routing conflicts.
B. Add virtual network peerings: Virtual WAN is designed to replace the need for manual virtual network peering.
F. Redeploy GW1: Application Gateway does not need to be re-deployed. You can point it to a new backend like backend pool that contains virtual machines deployed in the Virtual WAN network or leverage the Virtual WAN hub to direct traffic to on-premises or internet resources.
Supporting Documentation:
About Virtual WAN Virtual WAN FAQ

question:
You have an application named App1 that listens for incoming requests on a preconfigured group of 50 TCP ports and UDP ports.
You install App1 on 10 Azure virtual machines.
You need to implement load balancing for App1 across all the virtual machines. The solution must minimize the number of load balancing rules.
What should you include in the solution?
options: Azure Application Gateway V2 that has multiple listeners
Azure Standard Load Balancer that has Floating IP enabled
Azure Standard Load Balancer that has high availability (HA) ports enabled
Azure Application Gateway v2 that has multiple site hosting enabled
explanation:
The correct answer is C, Azure Standard Load Balancer with High Availability (HA) Ports enabled. Here's why:
HA Ports are specifically designed to simplify load balancing scenarios involving a large number of ports.
Instead of configuring individual load balancing rules for each of the 50 TCP and 50 UDP ports (totaling 100 rules), HA Ports automatically handle traffic for all ports. This satisfies the requirement to minimize the number of load balancing rules. By using HA Ports, only one load balancing rule needs to be configured, vastly simplifying the setup.
Azure Application Gateway (options A and D) is primarily designed for web application traffic (HTTP/HTTPS) and offers features such as Web Application Firewall (WAF), SSL termination, and URL-based routing. While it can handle some non-HTTP traffic, it is not the most efficient or cost-effective solution for balancing a wide range of TCP and UDP ports like in this scenario. Furthermore, Application Gateway would require multiple listeners or site configurations, increasing complexity and the number of rules.
Floating IP (option B) is related to allowing virtual machines to maintain a single public IP address even if they move between Azure infrastructure. While helpful in some network scenarios, it doesn't address the core requirement of minimizing load balancing rules across numerous ports. The number of rules needed remains the same.
HA Ports on Azure Standard Load Balancer are tailored for scenarios where an application needs to listen on a wide range of ports, making it the most efficient solution in this case.
For further research, you can refer to the official Microsoft documentation on Azure Load Balancer and HA Ports:
Azure Load Balancer documentation: Provides a comprehensive overview of Azure Load Balancer, including the Standard tier features.
HA Ports documentation: Explains the purpose and configuration of HA Ports in Azure Load Balancer.

question:
Note: This question is part of a series of questions that present the same scenario. Each question in the series contains a unique solution that might meet the stated goals. Some question sets might have more than one correct solution, while others might not have a correct solution.
After you answer a question in this section, you will NOT be able to return to it. As a result, these questions will not appear in the review screen.
You have two Azure virtual networks named Vnet1 and Vnet2.
You have a Windows 10 device named Client1 that connects to Vnet1 by using a Point-to-Site (P2S) IKEv2 VPN.
You implement virtual network peering between Vnet1 and Vnet2. Vnet1 allows gateway transit. Vnet2 can use the remote gateway.
You discover that Client1 cannot communicate with Vnet2.
You need to ensure that Client1 can communicate with Vnet2.
Solution: You resize the gateway of Vnet1 to a larger SKU.
Does this meet the goal?
explanation:
The proposed solution of resizing the Vnet1 gateway to a larger SKU does not address the core issue of Client1's inability to communicate with Vnet2. The problem stems from routing configurations and the limitations of Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN connections in Azure, particularly when used in conjunction with virtual network peering and gateway transit.
Here's a detailed breakdown:
1. Problem: Client1 connects to Vnet1 via P2S VPN. Virtual network peering exists between Vnet1 and Vnet2 with gateway transit enabled. Client1 cannot reach Vnet2.
2. Root Cause: P2S VPN clients typically receive routes only for the virtual network they connect to (Vnet1 in this case). They are not automatically provided routes to peered networks (Vnet2) through the gateway transit. The resizing of the gateway does not alter this fundamental routing behavior.
3. Why resizing the gateway is irrelevant: Gateway SKUs primarily influence throughput, the number of supported connections, and feature availability (like ExpressRoute coexistence). They do not automatically propagate routes to P2S clients for peered networks.
4. Correct Approach (Alternatives): To enable Client1 to communicate with Vnet2, several options are available, none of which involve simply resizing the gateway:
Custom Routes: Configure custom routes on Client1 (or push them via the P2S configuration) to explicitly direct traffic destined for Vnet2's address space through the Vnet1 gateway. This requires knowing Vnet2's CIDR block.
Azure Route Server (ARS): ARS simplifies route exchange between Azure VNets, SD-WAN appliances and ExpressRoute. It can be used to dynamically learn and propagate routes between Vnets including the Vnet behind P2S VPN.
Azure Virtual Network Gateway as a Router (Not Recommended for Production): While technically feasible, using the Vnet1 gateway as a router and manually configuring routing tables is complex and not recommended for production environments due to management overhead.
NAT (Not Recommended): Network Address Translation (NAT) could allow Client1 to access Vnet2, but it breaks end-to-end addressing and is generally discouraged.
5. Lack of Route Propagation: The P2S VPN gateway does not automatically advertise routes to the peered Vnet (Vnet2) to the connected clients (Client1). The P2S clients need to explicitly know about the peered Vnet's address space and how to reach it.
6. Gateway Transit Mechanism: Gateway transit allows Vnet2 to use the VPN gateway in Vnet1. It does not automatically configure the VPN clients connected to Vnet1 to route to Vnet2. The traffic from the P2S Client needs to have a route to Vnet2 via Vnet1 gateway.
7. Authoritative References:
About VPN Gateway settings: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway- about-vpn-gateway-settings Configure VPN gateway transit for virtual network peering: https://learn.microsoft.com/en- us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway-peering-gateway-transit Azure Route Server: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/route-server/ In conclusion, resizing the Vnet1 gateway does nothing to resolve the routing issue preventing Client1 from communicating with Vnet2. The problem requires a routing solution, like custom routes or Azure Route Server, to propagate routes to the P2S client. Therefore, the answer is definitively "No."

question:

What is the minimum number of listeners and routing rules you should configure? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. Listeners :2 A listener in Azure Application Gateway is responsible for accepting incoming requests based on a specified protocol (HTTP/HTTPS) and port.
Listeners bind to a frontend IP configuration and define how requests should be processed.
If there are two different frontend configurations (e.g., different domains, ports, or protocols), then two listeners are required.
2. Routing Rules : 2.
Routing rules define how the incoming requests should be processed after being received by a listener.
Each routing rule specifies:
The backend pool (where the request should be forwarded).
The path-based or host-based routing mechanism.
If there are two different routing rules, they might be set up to:
Route traffic based on different URLs (e.g., /api vs. /app).
Forward requests to different backend pools (e.g., WebApp1 and WebApp2).

question:

You need to ensure that the Branch1 users can connect to the Azure resources. The solution must meet the following requirements:
• Minimize downtime for all users.
• Minimize administrative effort.
What should you do first?
explanation:
C: Reset Connection1.
Minimize Downtime:
Resetting the connection (Connection1) typically involves a quick re-establishment of the connection between Branch1 and Azure resources. This action is likely to result in minimal downtime compared to other options like recreating or resetting other components.
Minimize Administrative Effort:
Resetting the connection is a straightforward administrative task. It doesn't involve recreating or reconfiguring larger components like gateways or routers, which would require more time and effort.
It also avoids the need for deeper troubleshooting or configuration changes that could introduce additional risks or complexity.

explanation:

An Azure Load Balancer is used to distribute incoming network traffic among multiple virtual machines within a Virtual Network (VNet).
It is commonly used for scalability and high availability of applications.
A load balancer is a network service typically associated with VNets to ensure traffic distribution among multiple backend virtual machines (VMs).

Private Endpoints allow resources such as Azure Storage, Azure SQL Database, and other PaaS services to be securely accessed from within a private virtual network.
Private Endpoints provide private IP addresses from within the virtual network, eliminating the need for public internet exposure.

It allows services to be accessed securely over a private IP address instead of public internet exposure.

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains the virtual networks shown in the following table.
You have a virtual machine named VM5 that has the following IP address configurations:
• IP address:10.4.0.5 • Subnet mask:255.255.255.0 • Default gateway: 10.4.0.1 • DNS server: 168.63.129.16 You have an Azure Private DNS zone named fabrikam.com that contains the records shown in the following table.
The virtual network links in the fabrikam.com DNS zone are configured as shown in the exhibit. (Click the Exhibit tab.) VM5 fails to resolve the IP address for app1.fabrikam.com.
For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. Updating the IP address configurations of VM5 to use a DNS server address of 10.4.0.2 will enable the virtual machine to resolve app1.fabrikam.com.
NO Setting a custom DNS server IP (10.4.0.2) does not automatically guarantee name resolution unless:
The DNS server at 10.4.0.2 has a record for app1.fabrikam.com.
The virtual network is correctly linked to the Private DNS Zone.
2. Enabling a virtual network link for Vnet3 in the fabrikam.com DNS zone will enable VM5 to resolve app1.fabrikam.com.
YES In Azure Private DNS, a virtual machine can only resolve DNS records in a Private DNS Zone if its virtual network is linked to that zone.
If VM5 is inside Vnet3, and Vnet3 is linked to the fabrikam.com DNS zone, then VM5 can resolve app1.fabrikam.com.
Conclusion: If Vnet3 is linked to the zone, VM5 can resolve the DNS name.
3. Adding an A record for app1.fabrikam.com to the fabrikam.com DNS zone will enable VM5 to resolve app1.fabrikam.com.
NO Adding an A record is necessary for name resolution, but it alone is not sufficient.
The virtual machine must be in a VNet linked to the Private DNS Zone.
If VM5 is in a VNet that is not linked, it will not resolve the DNS name even if the A record exists.

question:
Your company has five offices. Each office has a firewall device and a local internet connection. The offices connect to a third-party SD-WAN.
You have an Azure subscription that contains a virtual network named Vnet1. Vnet1 contains a virtual network gateway named Gateway1. Each office connects to Gateway1 by using a Site-to-Site VPN connection.
You need to replace the third-party SD-WAN with an Azure Virtual WAN.
What should you include in the solution?
options: Create new Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN connections on the firewall devices.
Create an Azure Traffic Manager profile.
explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why deleting Gateway1 is the correct initial step when migrating from a Site- to-Site VPN setup to Azure Virtual WAN:
The existing network infrastructure utilizes a hub-and-spoke topology with Gateway1 acting as the VPN gateway within the Vnet1 hub, connecting the five offices via Site-to-Site VPN tunnels established through their individual firewalls and a third-party SD-WAN. The goal is to replace this setup with Azure Virtual WAN, which provides a centralized network management platform for connecting branch offices and remote users to Azure. Virtual WAN inherently provides its own VPN gateway functionality within the Virtual Hub.
Given the current configuration using Site-to-Site VPNs with Gateway1, transitioning to Virtual WAN necessitates removing the existing Azure VPN gateway (Gateway1) to avoid conflicts and ensure proper routing within the new Virtual WAN environment. Virtual WAN essentially handles the creation and management of VPN gateways within its virtual hubs, negating the need for the pre-existing Gateway1. You're essentially migrating the VPN gateway functionality from a VNet gateway (Gateway1) to a Virtual WAN hub gateway.
Option B, creating new Point-to-Site VPN connections on the firewall devices, is incorrect because Point-to- Site VPNs are designed for individual user connections, not site-to-site connectivity between offices and a central Azure hub. Option C, creating an Azure Traffic Manager profile, is irrelevant as Traffic Manager focuses on load balancing and traffic routing across different Azure regions or endpoints, not on establishing core network connectivity between branch offices and Azure. Option D, enabling active-active mode on Gateway1, might seem relevant for redundancy, but it doesn't address the fundamental requirement of migrating to the Virtual WAN architecture. Gateway1 is being replaced by Virtual WAN’s built-in gateway capabilities.
Therefore, the first crucial step is to dismantle the previous VPN architecture centered around Gateway1, making option A the correct initial action for a clean and conflict-free migration to Azure Virtual WAN. After deleting Gateway1, you would proceed to create an Azure Virtual WAN, connect the branch offices using Virtual WAN's Site-to-Site VPN functionality, and configure routing policies within the Virtual WAN hub.
Supporting links:
Azure Virtual WAN Documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-wan/virtual-wan-about Virtual WAN Architecture: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-wan/virtual-wan-faq#what-is-the- architecture-of-virtual-wan Migrate to Virtual WAN: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-wan/how-to-migrate-hub-spoke

question:
You are planning the IP addressing for the subnets in Azure virtual networks.
Which type of resource requires IP addresses in the subnets?
options: Azure DDoS Protection for virtual networks
explanation:
The correct answer is A, internal load balancers. Here's why:
Internal load balancers (ILBs) operate within a virtual network (VNet). They require a private IP address from a subnet to serve as their front-end IP. This IP address is the entry point for services within the VNet. When traffic is sent to this IP address, the load balancer distributes it among the backend instances (e.g., VMs) in the configured backend pool. This assignment of a private IP allows services within the VNet to access the ILB and its distributed applications.
Azure DDoS Protection for virtual networks (option B) doesn't directly consume IP addresses within your subnets. It's a service that provides protection against distributed denial-of-service attacks and is applied at the VNet level, operating mostly outside the subnets themselves.
Service endpoints (option D) enable secure and direct access to Azure services from your VNet. Service endpoint policies (option C) further refine this access. While these endpoints interact with subnets, they don't require IP addresses within the subnet to be assigned to the endpoint itself. The resources accessing services via service endpoints still need IPs, but the endpoint itself doesn't "consume" an IP from the subnet's address space. The subnet trusts certain service endpoints, and traffic destined for these service endpoints is then routed optimally via the Azure backbone network without going through the public internet.
In summary, the essential functionality of an internal load balancer is tied to a specific IP within a subnet to function properly.
Supporting Links:
Azure Load Balancer: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/ Internal Load Balancer: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/load-balancer-internal Azure DDoS Protection: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ddos-protection/ Service Endpoints: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network-service- endpoints-overview

explanation:
Step 1: Navigate to Azure Virtual Networks Open the Azure portal.
Go to Virtual Networks.
Step 2: Select the Virtual Network Choose the VNet containing the subnet you want to modify.
Step 3: Access Subnet Settings Under Settings, click Subnets.
Step 4: Select the Target Subnet Choose the subnet to be modified (e.g., subnet 4-3).
Step 5: Modify Subnet Address Range The number of required hosts determines the CIDR notation of the subnet.
Example Scenario:
If 507 hosts are required, a 9-bit address range is necessary.
The correct subnet size is /23 (512 addresses).
Example: 10.0.0.0/23.
Subnet Sizing Considerations Azure reserves certain IPs in every subnet:
First and last address for protocol conformance.
Three additional addresses for Azure service usage.
The smallest subnet range allowed in Azure is /29 (8 IPs total, 3 usable).
Example of subnet allocations:
/22 → 1024 IPs /23 → 512 IPs /24 → 256 IPs /29 → 8 IPs (only 3 usable).
Step 6: Save Changes Click Save to apply the new subnet address range.
Why is this Important?
Ensures enough IPs for network resources.
Prevents IP exhaustion in growing networks.
Optimizes Azure services that rely on subnet IP allocation.

question:
You are planning the IP addressing for the subnets in Azure virtual networks.
Which type of resource requires IP addresses in the subnets?
options: Azure DDoS Protection for virtual networks
explanation:
Internal load balancers in Azure require IP addresses from the subnet in which they are deployed to function correctly. This is because an internal load balancer needs an IP address to serve as its frontend, allowing traffic to be distributed to the backend pool of virtual machines within that virtual network. The frontend IP address acts as the access point for services running on the backend VMs. The subnet provides the range of IP addresses from which the load balancer's frontend IP can be allocated, making IP address availability a crucial prerequisite for its deployment. Without a valid IP address within the subnet, the internal load balancer cannot accept and distribute traffic.
Azure DDoS Protection plans, service endpoint policies, and service endpoints themselves do not directly consume IP addresses from the subnet in the same manner as an internal load balancer. DDoS Protection protects the entire virtual network but operates at a higher level than individual IP addresses within subnets.
Service endpoint policies and service endpoints secure and route traffic to Azure services (like Azure Storage or Azure SQL Database) without assigning IP addresses to a specific resource within the subnet. They define access control and network routing rules but don't require dedicated IPs from the subnet's address space for their core function. Service endpoints utilize the virtual network's address space but do not directly consume specific IP addresses within the subnets for their operation. Instead, they leverage network routes and firewall rules based on service tags.
Therefore, only internal load balancers necessitate IP addresses from the subnet's range, making it the correct answer. Other options focus on network security and connectivity at a different layer of abstraction.
Relevant Documentation:
Azure Load Balancer: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/ Azure Virtual Network service endpoints: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual- network-service-endpoints-overview Azure DDoS Protection: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ddos-protection/ddos-protection-overview

explanation:
both Subnet1 and Subnet2.
A NAT Gateway can be linked to multiple subnets in a Virtual Network (VNet).
It allows outbound internet traffic for resources in those subnets to use a static public IP address or a range of public IPs.
The only restriction is that a NAT Gateway cannot be linked to a GatewaySubnet (used for VPN and ExpressRoute).
16 IP addresses.

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains a virtual network named VNet1. VNet1 contains the resources shown in the following table.
You need to publish App1 by using AG1 and a URL of https://app1.contoso.com. The solution must meet the following requirements:
•TLS connections must terminate on AG1.
•Minimize the number of targets in the backend pool of AG1.
•Minimize the number of deployed copies of the SSL certificate of App1.
How many locations should you import to the certificate, and how many targets should you add to the backend pool of AG1? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.

explanation:

question:
You are planning the IP addressing for the subnets in Azure virtual networks.
Which type of resource requires IP addresses in the subnets?
explanation:
The correct answer is B, internal load balancers, because they require an IP address within the subnet to act as the frontend IP for the load balancer. This IP address is used by clients to connect to the load balancer, which then distributes traffic to the backend pool of virtual machines or other resources.
Storage accounts do not directly consume IP addresses within a subnet. While storage accounts have public endpoints by default, and can be integrated with virtual networks using service endpoints or private endpoints, the service endpoints (option C) themselves don't consume IPs, they configure the storage account and the network to allow traffic to securely pass between them. Private endpoints, however, do consume IPs but aren't listed as an option. Thus, option C is still incorrect.
Virtual network peering (option D) enables connectivity between virtual networks but does not require individual IP addresses within the subnets being peered. Peering establishes a link that allows traffic to flow between the address spaces of the peered networks. Peering relies on routing configurations and network policies.
Internal load balancers need a dedicated IP address to function as the entry point for traffic within the virtual network. They act as a central point of contact and distribute traffic across the backend servers. Without an IP address assigned to the internal load balancer, it cannot be reached, and traffic cannot be routed. The IP address sits in the same subnet of the resources it is balancing, and is considered part of that subnet's address space.
Here's an explanation using cloud computing concepts: Internal load balancers act as a network service within the virtual network, requiring an IP address to be discoverable and reachable. This IP address is essential for the load balancer's role in distributing traffic among backend resources.
For more information, refer to the following Microsoft Azure documentation:
Azure Load Balancer: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/ Azure Virtual Network Peering: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network- peering-overview Azure Storage Service Endpoints: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage- network-security

question:
You have an Azure subscription that contains a virtual network named VNet1.
You deploy several web apps and configure the apps to use private endpoints on VNet1.
You need to identify which DNS records the web apps registered automatically.
Where will the records be created?
options: an Azure DNS zone named privatelink.azurewebsites.net
an Azure Private DNS zone named azurewebsites.net
an Azure Private DNS zone named privatelink.azurewebsites.net
an Azure DNS zone named azurewebsites.net
explanation:
The correct answer is C. an Azure Private DNS zone named privatelink.azurewebsites.net.
When you create a private endpoint for Azure services, including web apps, a private DNS zone is used to resolve the private IP addresses of the service endpoints within your virtual network. These DNS zones allow your VMs and other resources within the VNet to reach the services using their private IPs, bypassing the public internet.
For Azure Web Apps, the naming convention for the private DNS zone is privatelink.azurewebsites.net. Azure automatically creates and manages these DNS records in the private DNS zone associated with the private endpoint. This ensures that when a web app uses the private endpoint, DNS resolution for the web app's hostname (e.g., myapp.azurewebsites.net) within VNet1 resolves to the private IP address associated with the private endpoint, instead of the public IP address. This keeps the traffic within the Azure network.
Options A and D are incorrect because azurewebsites.net is not the naming convention used for private DNS zones related to private endpoints. Option B is incorrect because while Azure Private DNS zones are used, the specific zone for web apps is named privatelink.azurewebsites.net to identify it as a private link DNS zone.
Azure DNS zones are typically for public resolution and not private resolution within a VNet, hence they would not contain the records created for Private Endpoints.Further Reading:
Azure Private Link DNS integration: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint- dns Configure Azure DNS Private Zones: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/dns/private-dns-getstarted- portal

question:
You are planning the IP addressing for the subnets in Azure virtual networks.
Which type of resource requires IP addresses in the subnets?
explanation:
The correct answer is B. internal load balancers.
Internal load balancers (ILBs) in Azure require IP addresses from the subnet in which they are deployed. The ILB's frontend IP configuration necessitates an IP address within the virtual network subnet. This IP address serves as the single point of contact for applications within the virtual network.
Storage accounts, service endpoints, and service endpoint policies do not directly consume IP addresses within subnets in the same manner as internal load balancers. While storage accounts have public endpoints, they don't necessitate a specific IP address reservation within a subnet. Service endpoints provide secure and direct connectivity to Azure services by extending a virtual network's private address space, but they themselves don't require dedicated subnet IP addresses. Service endpoint policies are used to filter outbound traffic to service endpoints and also do not consume IP addresses. Therefore, option B is the correct answer because deploying an internal load balancer within a subnet requires an IP address from that subnet to serve as its private frontend IP.
For further information, refer to the official Microsoft Azure documentation on Azure Load Balancer:
Azure Load Balancer Documentation

question:
You plan to implement an Azure virtual network that will contain 10 virtual subnets. The subnets will use IPv6 addresses. Each subnet will host up to 200 load-balanced virtual machines.
You need to recommend which subnet mask size to use for the virtual subnets.
What should you recommend?
explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for recommending a /64 subnet mask for the Azure virtual network subnets using IPv6, hosting up to 200 load-balanced virtual machines each:
Azure, by default, allocates a /64 IPv6 address space to each subnet within a virtual network. This is a deliberate design decision by Microsoft to ensure adequate address space and simplify network management.
While you could technically carve out smaller IPv6 subnets, it's generally not recommended within Azure, primarily due to this design principle and the way Azure handles IPv6 addressing.
A /64 subnet provides an incredibly large address space (2^64 addresses). This is more than sufficient for hosting 200 VMs and providing room for growth and internal services within each subnet. Attempting to use a smaller subnet (like /120) would mean the subnet's prefix may conflict with the /64 subnet that Azure assigned, thus causing routing issues.
Using a larger subnet (like /48 or /24) is unnecessary and inefficient for this scenario. A /48 is typically reserved for large organizations or entire sites, while /24 is still too large for a single subnet that only needs to accommodate a few hundred machines. Allocating more addresses than needed leads to address space wastage. Also, it's crucial to note that while the question does not explicitly indicate an issue utilizing a larger subnet, it is best practice to efficiently use IP address space and to design according to Azure best practices.
The implicit requirement is the most efficient address use.
Therefore, the /64 default allocation is the optimal and recommended approach. It provides ample addresses, aligns with Azure's design principles, and avoids unnecessary address space allocation.
overviewhttps://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-faq - See section on IPv6.

question:
- You have two on-premises datacenters.
You have an Azure subscription that contains four virtual networks named VNet1, VNet2, VNet3, and VNet4.
You create an Azure virtual WAN named VWAN1. VWAN1 contains a single virtual hub that is connected to both on- premises datacenters and all the virtual networks in a full mesh topology.
You create a route table named RT1.
You need to configure VWAN1 to meet the following requirements:
•Connectivity between VNet1 and VNet2 and both on-premises datacenters must be allowed.
•Connectivity between VNet3 and VNet4 and both on-premises datacenters must be allowed.
•VNet1 and VNet2 must be isolated from VNet3 and VNet4.
How should you configure routing for VNet1 and VNet2 and for both on-premises datacenters? To answer, drag the appropriate route tables and route table propagation to the correct requirements. Each route table and route table propagation may be used once, more than once, or not at all. You may need to drag the split bar between panes or scroll to view content.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. VNet1 and VNet2 Associated route table (RT1): These VNets will look at RT1 whenever they want to send traffic. They do not look at the Default route table.
Propagating to (Default): These VNets "shout" their address prefix into the Default route table. This means anything associated with the Default table (like your On-premises) will know how to find them.
2. On-premises datacenters Associated route table (Default): On-premises traffic will look at the Default route table to decide where to go.
Propagating to (RT1 and Default): On-premises "shouts" its address prefix into both tables. This ensures that resources in both RT1 and Default know how to reach the datacenter.

question:
You are planning the IP addressing for the subnets in Azure virtual networks.
Which type of resource requires IP addresses in the subnets?
explanation:
The correct answer is indeed C, internal load balancers. Here's why:
An internal load balancer (ILB) in Azure requires IP addresses from the subnet where it's deployed because it must have an IP address within the virtual network's address space. This IP address serves as the internal endpoint for applications or services behind the load balancer. Clients within the same virtual network can use this IP address to access the load-balanced resources.
Let's analyze the other options:
A. Azure Virtual Network NAT: While Azure Virtual Network NAT is used to provide outbound internet connectivity for resources within a virtual network, it does not directly require IP addresses from the subnet for its own operation. It uses a public IP associated with the NAT gateway. The resources using the NAT gateway consume IP addresses from the subnet, but the NAT gateway itself does not.
B. Service Endpoint Policies: Service endpoint policies are used to filter egress traffic to Azure services using service endpoints. They don't require dedicated IP addresses within the subnet for their operation. They work by restricting the traffic based on the defined rules.
D. Virtual Network Peering: Virtual network peering enables you to seamlessly connect two or more virtual networks in Azure. Peering itself doesn't consume IP addresses directly within a subnet. Resources in the peered networks use their own assigned IPs from their respective subnets.
In summary, an internal load balancer needs an internal IP address from a subnet so that clients in the same virtual network can access the applications or services behind the load balancer.
For further reading, you can refer to the official Microsoft Azure documentation on Internal Load Balancers:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/load-balancer-internal. You can also read about Azure Virtual Network NAT here: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/nat-gateway/nat-overview and Service Endpoints here: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network-service- endpoints-overview and Virtual Network Peering here: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual- network/virtual-network-peering-overview

•The user traffic of App1 will be managed by using Azure Front Door.
•The traffic between Front Door and the App Service apps will be sent by using HTTP.

You need to support the Front Door deployment.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. DNS Records: An A record and a CNAME record.
A Record (Address Record):
This maps a domain name (e.g., example.com) to an IP address (IPv4).
Used for direct domain-to-IP mapping.
CNAME Record (Canonical Name Record):
This maps a domain alias to another domain name rather than an IP address.
Commonly used to create subdomains (e.g., www.example.com pointing to example.com).
A records are required when you want a domain to resolve directly to an IP.
CNAME records are useful for pointing a domain to another domain without using an IP.
This is a common setup for web hosting and domain name management.
2. Import the certificate to: Vault1 This likely refers to Azure Key Vault, a cloud service used to store and manage secrets, including SSL/TLS certificates.
Azure Key Vault allows secure management of cryptographic keys and certificates.
Certificates stored in Key Vault can be used by Azure services, such as App Services and Virtual Machines.
Centralized security and policy enforcement.
Alternative options in the dropdown:
The app registration for App1: Would be used for identity management.
The App Service apps: Would be for direct use in Azure Web Apps.
Storing a certificate in Key Vault provides enhanced security and centralized management.
Certificates can be accessed by multiple services securely.
Useful for automated certificate renewal and policy enforcement.

question:
You have an Azure subscription that contains a virtual network named VNet1. VNet1 has a subnet mask of/24.
You plan to implement an Azure application gateway that will have the following configurations:
•Public endpoints: 1 •Private endpoints: 1 •Minimum instances: 1 •Maximum instances: 10 You need to configure the address space for the subnet of the application gateway. The solution must minimize the number of IP addresses allocated to the application gateway subnet.
What is the minimum number of assignable IP addresses required?
explanation:
The minimum number of assignable IP addresses required for the application gateway subnet is 11.
Here's why:
Azure Application Gateway requires a dedicated subnet. While it might seem like only the configured instances would need IPs, Azure reserves some addresses within the subnet for its own management and operational needs.
Specifically, Azure reserves five IP addresses within each subnet for its internal use. This is a standard Azure networking behavior to ensure proper functionality of the platform.
In this scenario, the application gateway has a minimum of 1 instance. This means we need at least one IP address for the gateway itself, even when scaled down.
Further, the question states there will be one public endpoint and one private endpoint. Azure Application Gateway v2 (the version typically deployed now) can handle both public and private endpoints on a single gateway instance. This doesn't increase the IP address requirements for the gateway's instances themselves, but is a factor in the overall subnet design.
The maximum number of instances is 10. While this sets the upper limit for scaling, we only need to consider the minimum required addresses for this particular question.
Therefore, the calculation is as follows:
Azure reserved addresses: 5 Minimum instances: 1 (even if scaled down to 1 instance, the gateway still requires at least one IP) Total required = 5 (reserved) + 1 (minimum instance) = 6.
However the minimum instances must be greater than 1 which means the subnet must accomodate at least 2 IP addresses, Revised Total required = 5 (reserved) + 2 (minimum instance) = 7 For the instance to work, it must have at least a public address, and a private address to communicate inside the VNET. The calculation becomes.Revised Total required = 5 (reserved) + 2 (minimum instance - internal subnet) + 1 for public = 8 If we have 1 public endpoint, one private endpoint, and a minimum of 1 gateway instance, then we also need two other IP addresses.
In addition we have to consider the instance size. If the size is small then 2 instances will be required.
So, with 1 public and 1 private, the subnet requires at least 2 IP addresses at all times. The updated calculation is 5+2 = 7 IP Addresses.
Adding to all these consideration, there must also be at least 4 assignable addresses for the public or private endpoints. 2 * public and private.
In addition to all this, Azure sometimes requires additional IP addresses for updates.Therefore, the bare minimum is 5 (reserved) + 2 (min. instances) = 7 addresses + an addition 4 for public/private endpoints = 11 or more However, let's break down the official Azure guidance:
Reserved Addresses: Azure reserves the first four and the last IP addresses in each subnet.
Application Gateway v2: Requires a dedicated subnet.
The critical aspect is that Azure's internal use plus the gateway itself need IP addresses.
Final Calculation:
5 (Azure reserved) + 6 (for the public and private endpoints + instances running) = 11.
The best available answer to this question is: C. 11 Supporting Documentation:
Azure Virtual Network FAQ: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-faq Azure Application Gateway sizing: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/application- gateway-autoscaling-zone-redundant#size

question:
- Your on-premises network contains a server named DNS1 that runs Windows Server 2022. DNS1 has the DNS server role and an IP address of 10.1.0.1. The network contains computers that use DNS1 for name resolution.
You have an Azure subscription that contains the resources shown in the following table.

You need to ensure that the computers on the on-premises network can resolve the IP address for sql1.private.fabrikam.com.
What should you do on DNS1 and DNS2? To answer, drag the appropriate actions to the correct servers. Each action may be used once, more than once, or not at all. You may need to drag the split bar between panes or scroll to view content.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. DNS1: Configure forwarding to 168.63.129.16.
This is a well-known Azure internal recursive resolver used in Azure Virtual Networks (VNet).
It provides name resolution for VMs and resources within Azure without needing a custom DNS setup.

- Case Study - This is a case study. Case studies are not timed separately. You can use as much exam time as you would like to complete each case. However, there may be additional case studies and sections on this exam. You must manage your time to ensure that you are able to complete all questions included on this exam in the time provided.

Overview - Contoso, Ltd. is a consulting company that has a main office in San Francisco and a branch office in Dallas.
Contoso recently purchased an Azure subscription and is performing its first pilot project in Azure.

The Azure subscription contains the virtual networks shown in the following table.
Vnet1 contains a virtual network gateway named GW1.
Azure Virtual Machines - The Azure subscription contains virtual machines that run Windows Server 2019 as shown in the following table.
The NSGs are associated to the network interfaces on the virtual machines. Each NSG has one custom security rule that allows RDP connections from the internet. The firewall on each virtual machine allows ICMP traffic.
An application security group named ASG1 is associated to the network interface of VM1.
Azure Network Infrastructure Diagram Azure Private DNS Zones - The Azure subscription contains the Azure private DNS zones shown in the following table.
Zone1.contoso.com has the virtual network links shown in the following table.
Other Azure Resources - The Azure subscription contains additional resources as shown in the following table.
Requirements - Virtual Network Requirements - Contoso has the following virtual network requirements:
•Create a virtual network named Vnet6 in West US that will contain the following resources and configurations:
o Two container groups that connect to Vnet6 o Three virtual machines that connect to Vnet6 o Allow VPN connections to be established to Vnet6 o Allow the resources in Vnet6 to access KeyVault1, DB1, and Vnet1 over the Microsoft backbone network.
•The virtual machines in Vnet4 and Vnet5 must be able to communicate over the Microsoft backbone network.
•A virtual machine named VM-Analyze will be deployed to Subnet1. VM-Analyze must inspect the outbound network traffic from Subnet2 to the internet.
Network Security Requirements - Contoso has the following network security requirements:
•Configure Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) authentication for Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN users.
•Enable NSG flow logs for NSG3 and NSG4.
•Create an NSG named NSG10 that will be associated to Vnet1/Subnet1 and will have the custom inbound security rules shown in the following table.
•Create an NSG named NSG11 that will be associated to Vnet1/Subnet2 and will have the custom outbound security rules shown in the following table.
Which virtual machines can VM1 and VM4 ping successfully before NSG10 and NSG11 are created? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
VM1: VM2, VM3, and VM4 only.
This selection means that VM1 can communicate with VM2, VM3, and VM4, but not with VM5.
The possible reasons for this connectivity setup could be:
Network Security Groups (NSGs): Configured firewall rules allowing traffic between these VMs while restricting VM5.
Virtual Network (VNet) Peering: VM5 might be in a different subnet or VNet with no peering enabled.
Subnet Configuration: VM5 could be in a different subnet with restricted routing.
VM4: VM1, VM2, and VM3 only.
This selection means that VM4 can communicate with VM1, VM2, and VM3, but not VM5.
Similar to VM1's case, the restriction on VM5 could be due to:
NSG rules restricting access.
Subnet segmentation where VM5 is placed in an isolated subnet.
Routing configurations preventing VM5 from reaching VM1, VM2, or VM3.

explanation:

Resources connected to the virtual networks must be able to resolve DNS names for fabrikam.com.
This requires a DNS resolver configured to handle queries for fabrikam.com.

This requires a separate DNS resolver configured to handle queries for contoso.com.
The solution must minimize costs and administrative effort.
While minimizing costs and effort is important, the requirement to resolve DNS names for two different domains (fabrikam.com and contoso.com) typically necessitates separate resolvers unless conditional forwarding or a more complex setup is used, which could increase administrative effort.
Azure Dns private resolver is a regional service. "A DNS resolver can only refer to a virtual network that's in the same region as the DNS resolver"

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains a dual-stack virtual network named VNet1. VNet1 has the following IP address spaces:
•IPv4: 192.168.0.0/24 •IPv6: fd00:db8:deca:deed:/48 You plan to deploy an Azure VPN gateway and multiple virtual machines to VNet1.
You need to configure the subnet masks for VNet1. The solution must meet the following requirements:
•Maximize the number of usable IP addresses.
•Support the deployment of the VPN gateway and the virtual machines.
Which subnet mask should you use for each address space? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
IPv4 :/24 IPv4 addresses use a subnet mask to determine how many bits are allocated to the network and how many are for host identification.
A /24 subnet mask means that 24 bits are allocated to the network portion, leaving 8 bits for host addresses.
Subnet Mask: 255.255.255.0 Total IPs in the Subnet: 256 (2⁸) Usable IPs: 254 (since 2 addresses are reserved: network address (first IP) and broadcast address (last IP)) Use Case: /24 is a standard subnet size commonly used in enterprise networks, virtual networks, and cloud environments because it provides a sufficient number of IPs.
IPv6 : /64 IPv6 does not use traditional subnet masks like IPv4 but instead defines subnets using prefix lengths.
A /64 subnet means that the first 64 bits represent the network prefix, while the remaining 64 bits are used for host addresses.
Total IPs in the Subnet: 2¹²⁸-⁶⁴ = 2⁶⁴ (18 quintillion IPs!) Use Case: /64 is the standard subnet size for IPv6 networks, as it supports SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration), making it ideal for most deployments, including cloud and enterprise networks.

You have an Azure subscription. The subscription contains a locally-redundant storage (LRS) account named storage1 that is deployed to the US East Azure region and has a Microsoft.Storage service endpoint.

The solution must minimize administrative effort.
What should you do first?
options: Create an object replication rule for storage.
From storage1, select Secure transfer required.
explanation:
B. Delete the existing service endpoint.
You originally created a service endpoint for Microsoft.Storage when the storage account was LRS (local region only).
After upgrading to RA-GRS, the storage account now has:
Primary region: East US Secondary paired region: East US 2 (read-only) But service endpoints are region-bound at creation time.
Because the endpoint was created when the storage account was LRS, it only supports the primary region.
To allow access to the secondary region (paired region) using service endpoints:
You must remove the old service endpoint and recreate it, so Azure recognizes both regions.
This is the minimum administrative effort and the required step per Microsoft documentation.

question:

Without this step, Azure resources cannot use the ExpressRoute circuit.

question:

What is the minimum number of DDoS Network Protection plans you should deploy?
explanation:
B. 2.
Each virtual network contains 20 internet-accessible resources with public IP addresses.
These resources need protection from DDoS attacks.
The solution must minimize costs.
Azure DDoS Network Protection plans are billed per protected public IP address, and the cost can be optimized by sharing a single DDoS protection plan across multiple resources within the same virtual network.
Azure DDoS Network Protection plans can cover multiple public IP addresses within the same virtual network.
However, if there are multiple virtual networks, each virtual network typically requires its own DDoS protection plan to ensure comprehensive coverage and isolation.

explanation:

The selection of "3" likely refers to an existing subnet or a specific subnet count configuration.

question:
You have an Azure subscription that contains 100 network security groups (NSGs).
You need to ensure that you log the application of specific NSG rules.
Which type of log should you configure?
explanation:
The correct answer is C. Azure resource log. Here's a detailed justification:
To log the application of specific NSG rules, you need a log that captures details about the operations performed on the NSG itself. Azure resource logs are designed to provide this level of detail, capturing operational and diagnostic information for Azure resources, including network security groups. This includes events related to NSG rule processing.
Azure resource logs provide insights into operations that were performed within an Azure resource. For NSGs, this can include information about when rules were applied, modified, or deleted.
Flow logs capture information about IP traffic flowing through an NSG, which is useful for network traffic analysis and security monitoring, but do not log the application of the rules themselves.
Activity logs track subscription-level events, such as creating or deleting an NSG, but they do not provide granular details about the application of specific rules within the NSG.
Audit logs are generally considered to be part of the Activity Log in Azure and, therefore, do not offer the granular rule application information required.
Therefore, to audit the successful or unsuccessful application of specific NSG rules to network traffic, Azure resource logs are the appropriate choice.
Authoritative Links:
Azure Resource Logs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/essentials/resource-logs Network Security Group Logging with Azure Network Watcher: https://learn.microsoft.com/en- us/azure/network-watcher/network-watcher-logging-overview - While focused on Network Watcher, this page touches upon NSG logging capabilities.

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains a virtual machine named VM1 and a virtual network named Vnet1.

You plan to deploy a new virtual machine named VM2 that will perform network traffic routing and inspection.

NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. Deploying VM2 to: Gateway Subnet.
Gateway Subnet is reserved for VPN Gateways and Azure Virtual Network Gateways, and VMs cannot be deployed in this subnet.
However, if this is an exception scenario, Azure may allow placing a VM in GatewaySubnet for specific network services, such as:
Network Virtual Appliances (NVA) Custom VPN solutions Firewall appliances Diagnostics and monitoring VMs (for troubleshooting gateway traffic) 2. Associating the Custom Route Table with: Subnet1.
Custom route tables are used to control network traffic flow.
Associating a custom route table with Subnet1 allows:
Routing traffic through an NVA Forcing traffic through a VPN Gateway Directing internet-bound traffic to a firewall or proxy This is common in hybrid network scenarios where Subnet1 needs custom routing rules.

explanation:
1. Virtual Machine :Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition.
Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition is specifically designed for Azure-based workloads and offers cloud-native capabilities such as:
Hotpatching (install security updates without requiring a reboot).
Azure Automanage for streamlined VM management.
Extended support for Azure hybrid services.
This edition is optimized for running in Azure environments rather than on-premises.
2.Tool : Windows Admin Center.
Windows Admin Center (WAC) is a browser-based management tool used to remotely manage Windows Servers, including Azure-based servers.
It provides a modern GUI for managing server roles, networking, and security settings without requiring Remote Desktop.
WAC is useful for hybrid cloud management and integrates natively with Azure services.

question:
Your company has a remote office that contains a macOS device named Device1. Device1 has an IKEv2 VPN client installed.
You have an Azure subscription that contains the resources shown in the following table.
You need to ensure that Device1 can access the resources on VNet1 by using the VPN connections of VPNGW1. The solution must minimize administrative effort.
What should you do first?
options: To VNet1, deploy a virtual machine that contains a RADIUS server.
On Device1, install the OpenVPN client.
On Device1, add an X.509 certificate.
From Devices in the Microsoft Entra admin center, configure the Device settings.
explanation:
B. On Device1, install the OpenVPN client.
You need to ensure Device1 can access resources on VNet1 using the VPN connections of VPNGW1 while minimizing administrative effort.
Azure Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN supports three authentication methods:
Azure Certificate Authentication RADIUS Authentication Microsoft Entra ID Authentication (Previously Azure AD authentication) To connect to an Azure VPN Gateway (VPNGW1) from a client device (Device1), the user must install a VPN client that is compatible with the VPN gateway configuration.
Azure VPN Gateway supports multiple VPN protocols:
OpenVPN (SSL-based, recommended for cross-platform) IKEv2 (Windows native VPN client) SSTP (for Windows-only connections) Among these, OpenVPN is the most flexible and widely used, supporting Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android.

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains a virtual network named VNet1. VNet1 uses an IP address space of 192.168.0.0/24.
You plan to deploy Azure virtual machines and Azure Bastion to VNet1.
You need to recommend an IP subnetting configuration for VNet1. The solution must maximize the number of IP addresses that can be assigned to the virtual machines.
What should you recommend? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
/25 and 182 Because the total address space is /24 which gives 265 IPs. This number should be used to address both of the VM subnets and the Bastion subnet.
By default, Bastion subnets takes /26 which gives 64 IP address.
So the remaining IP addresses from the total address space is: 256-64 = 192 available IP address to be used for VMs.
But to use this IP range, needs to be divided into to VM Subnets:
/25 (128 - 5 = 123) /26 (64 - 5 = 59) Based on the above the total number of the available IP address for VM subnets is: 182 In the question its asking for the "Bit mask for the LARGER virtual machine subnet" which is: /25

- You have an on-premises network that includes the sites shown in the following table.
Each site is connected to the Internet by a firewall. All sites are connected to an SD-WAN. Each site is configured to propagate routes by using BGP.
You have an Azure subscription that includes a virtual network named Vnet1 that contains a Virtual Network Gateway named Gateway1.
You create a local network gateway with the configuration shown in the gateway exhibit (Click the Gateway tab.) You create a Site-to-Site (S2S) connection with the configuration shown in connection exhibit. (Click the Connection tab.) For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
Users in the Berlin site can connect to resources in Vnet1 via VPN1.
No: This statement is incorrect. Users in the Berlin site cannot connect to resources in Vnet1 via VPN1.
To create a direct Site-to-Site connection to the Berlin site, an additional Local Network Gateway is required.
Yes: This statement is correct. An additional Local Network Gateway is required to create a direct Site-to-Site connection to the Berlin site.
To enable users in the Paris site to connect to Vnet1, the IP address of LocalNetworkGateway1 must be changed to 172.16.0.1.
No: This statement is incorrect. Changing the IP address of LocalNetworkGateway1 to 172.16.0.1 is not necessary to enable users in the Paris site to connect to Vnet1.

- You have an on-premises server named Server that is assigned a public IP address of 131.107.100.200.
You have an Azure subscription that contains the resources shown in the following table.
storage85347 has the Networking settings configured as shown in the following exhibit.
From the Firewalls and virtual networks tab, you add Subnet1 to storage85347.
For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation: 1. A service endpoint is created on VNet1 : No.Service endpoints allow virtual networks to securely access Azure services without requiring a public IP address. However, service endpoints are not created automatically—they must be explicitly enabled on a subnet within a virtual network.Since the question does not specify that a service endpoint was manually configured on VNet1, this statement is false.2. All the virtual machines can access storage85347. :NoThe storage account storage85347 is not automatically accessible to all virtual machines (VMs) in the VNet unless explicit access rules (such as a service endpoint, private endpoint, or firewall rule) are configured.If no service endpoint, private endpoint, or explicit network access rule is set, the VMs may not have direct access to the storage account.The default configuration does not automatically grant access to all VMs.3. An additional route is created automatically in the route table of Subnet1 : NoIn Azure Virtual Networks, when a subnet is created, Azure provides default system routes (e.g., a route for the virtual network, internet, etc.).However, an additional route is not automatically created unless a user manually defines a custom route table and associates it with Subnet1.If no explicit configuration is mentioned, no extra route is automatically created.

question:
You have an on-premises datacenter in Seattle.
You have an Azure subscription that contains an Azure Network Watcher resource in the West US 2 Azure region.
You need to document network latency between the on-premises datacenter and the West US 2 region and between the on-premises datacenter and the East US 2 public Azure region. The solution must minimize administrative effort.
What should you do first?
options: Create a Network Watcher resource in the East US 2 region.
Create a Connection Monitor resource in the West US 2 region.
explanation:
The correct answer is D. Create a Connection Monitor resource in the West US 2 region.
Here's a detailed justification:
The primary goal is to document network latency between the on-premises datacenter and both West US 2 and East US 2 Azure regions with minimal administrative effort. Azure Network Watcher's Connection Monitor feature is designed precisely for this purpose.
Connection Monitor's Role: Connection Monitor proactively tests the connectivity and latency between different endpoints. It periodically sends probes (ICMP, TCP, or HTTP) between the source (on-premises datacenter, represented by an agent installed there) and destination (West US 2 or East US 2 Azure regions).
Why not A (Get-AzNetworkWatcherConnectionMonitor): This cmdlet retrieves information about an existing connection monitor. You need to create one first.
Why not B (Get-AzNetworkWatcherReachabilityProvidersList): This cmdlet provides a list of network providers and their routing paths to an Azure region. While useful for general understanding, it doesn't directly measure latency between the on-premises datacenter and Azure regions in an ongoing and documented manner.
Why not C (Create a Network Watcher resource in the East US 2 region): Network Watcher is automatically enabled in each region in your subscription. Creating a new Network Watcher resource is redundant and not necessary for creating connection monitors in that region. The default, enabled Network Watcher is sufficient.
You still need to create a Connection Monitor resource.
Connection Monitor Configuration: To measure latency, you need a Connection Monitor configured to monitor the connectivity between the on-premises endpoint and endpoints within the West US 2 and East US 2 regions. This involves configuring the Connection Monitor with source endpoint(s) (representing the on- premises datacenter) and destination endpoint(s) (representing resources in West US 2 and East US 2, such as VMs or virtual network gateways).
Single Connection Monitor for Multiple Destinations: A single Connection Monitor in West US 2 can monitor connectivity to both West US 2 and East US 2 destinations. It doesn't require one Connection Monitor per Azure region.
Minimal Effort: By using Connection Monitor, you automate the process of measuring and documenting latency. After initial setup, it continuously monitors the network and provides historical data.
Therefore, the first step is to create a Connection Monitor resource in the West US 2 region. Then, you would configure it to monitor the connectivity between the on-premises datacenter and both the West US 2 and East US 2 regions. This leverages Network Watcher's capabilities most efficiently to achieve the stated goal.
Authoritative Links:
Azure Network Watcher: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/network-watcher/network-watcher- monitoring-overview Connection Monitor: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/network-watcher/connection- monitor/connection-monitor-overview

- You have an Azure subscription that contains the virtual networks shown in the Virtual networks exhibit.
You create an Azure DNS Private Resolver as shown in the DNS private resolvers exhibit.
DNS-resolver-inbound-endpoint-1 is configured as shown in the Inbound endpoints exhibit.
DNS-resolver-rule-1 is configured as shown in the Rules exhibit.
For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
VNet1 can be linked to DNS-resolver-ruleset-1.
No you believe or know that VNet1 cannot be linked to "DNS-resolver-ruleset-1." This could be due to technical limitations in your system, a lack of configuration options, or a design choice.
VNet2 must be configured to use 10.3.1.4 as the preferred DNS server.
No Means you believe that VNet2 is not required to use 10.3.1.4 as its preferred DNS server. Perhaps it uses a different DNS server, obtains DNS server information dynamically, or has no specific requirement for this particular IP address.
Virtual machines connected to VNet3 can resolve names in the MargiesTravel.com domain.
Yes you believe or know that the DNS configuration for VNet3 (or the wider network infrastructure it relies on) is set up correctly to allow name resolution for the "MargiesTravel.com" domain. This could involve VNet3 being configured to use DNS servers that are authoritative for or can forward queries to the DNS servers responsible for "MargiesTravel.com."

To start the case study - To display the first question in this case study, click the Next button. Use the buttons in the left pane to explore the content of the case study before you answer the questions. Clicking these buttons displays information such as business requirements, existing environment, and problem statements. When you are ready to answer a question, click the Question button to return to the question.
Overview - Proseware, Inc. is a financial services company that has a main office in New York City and a branch office in San Francisco.
Existing Environment. Hybrid Environment Proseware has an on-premises Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) forest named corp.proseware.com that syncs with a Microsoft Entra tenant named proseware.com.
Proseware has an Azure subscription that is linked to proseware.com.
Proseware has an internal certification authority (CA).
Existing Environment. Network Infrastructure The offices contain the resources shown in the following table.
NYCNet connects to Azure by using an ExpressRoute circuit.
SFONet connects to Azure by using a Site-to-Site (S2S) VPN.
Existing Environment. Azure Resources The Azure subscription contains the virtual networks and subnets shown in the following table.
The subscription contains four virtual machines named VM1, VM2, VM3, and VM4. VM1 and VM2 host an app named App1.
VM3 and VM4 host a web app named App2 that is accessed by using a FQDN of app2.proseware.com. Users access app2.proseware.com by using HTTP or HTTPS.
VM1, VM2, and VM4 are connected to SpokeVNet.
The subscription contains Application Gateway resources shown in the following table.
The subscription contains an Azure Front Door Standard profile named FD1. FD1 contains a single origin group that targets APPGW1 by using the default endpoint name.
HubVNet connects to NYCNet by using an ExpressRoute gateway named ERGW1.
Planned Changes and Requirements. Planned Changes Proseware plans to implement the following changes:
•Deploy an Azure Private DNS Resolver named PRDNS1 to HubVNet and link PRDNS1 to SpokeVNet.
•Create a DNS forwarding ruleset named DNSRS1 and associate DNSRS1 with PRDNS1.
•Deploy Azure Virtual Network Manager and implement the following rules:
- Allow inbound connections on TCP port 3389 from the on-premises networks to SUBNET-JUMPHOSTS.
- Block inbound connections on TCP port 80 from the internet to SpokeVNet.
•Ensure that Azure Virtual Network Manager rules take precedence over conflicting NSG rules.
•Deploy two network virtual appliances (NVAs) named NVA1 and NVA2 to HubVNet.
•Deploy a gateway load balancer named LBGW1 to HubVNet.
•Configure LBGW1 to inspect traffic on TCP ports 443, 1433, and 1434 from LBS1 by using NVA1 and NVA2.
•Ensure that all the traffic to App2 is processed by using FD1.
Planned Changes and Requirements. Connectivity requirements Proseware identifies the following connectivity requirements:
•Minimize the complexity of the Azure Virtual Network Manager deployment.
•Route traffic between NYCNet and SFONet via the ExpressRoute circuit and the S2S VPN.
•Ensure that remote users on Windows 11 devices can connect to HubVNet by using a Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN and their proseware.com credentials.
Planned Changes and Requirements. Security requirements Proseware identifies the following security requirements:
•Whenever possible, use the internal CA.
•Ensure that all connections routed via APPGW1 use end-to-end encryption.
•Ensure that user connections to Azure-hosted apps use end-to-end encryption.
•Ensure that all inbound internet traffic to app2.proseware.com is routed via FD1.
•Prevent devices that connect to NYCNet from accessing Azure services that use private endpoints.
•Enable the virtual machines that connect to HubVNet and SpokeVNet to access Azure services that use private endpoints.
Planned Changes and Requirements. General requirements Proseware identifies the following general requirements:
•Minimize the IP address space required to deploy platform-managed resources to the virtual networks.
•From SpokeVNet, resolve name resolution requests for the azure.proseware.com namespace and the corp.proseware.com namespace by using PRDNS1.
•Whenever possible, minimize administrative effort.
You need to configure APPGW1 to support end-to-end encryption. The solution must meet the security requirements.
What should you do?
options: From the SSL settings, upload a TLS client certificate that is issued by the internal root CA and includes the full certificate chain.
From the Backend settings, upload the internal root CA certificate.
From the SSL settings, upload a TLS client certificate that is issued by the internal root CA.
From the Backend settings, upload a wildcard TLS certificate that has a private key issued by the internal root CA.
explanation:
B. From the Backend settings, upload the internal root CA certificate.
To enable end-to-end TLS encryption on Azure Application Gateway, the gateway must be able to trust the backend server certificates. Since the backend servers use certificates issued by an internal root CA, Application Gateway must be configured to trust that CA.

•From SpokeVNet, resolve name resolution requests for the azure.proseware.com namespace and the corp.proseware.com namespace by using PRDNS1.
•Whenever possible, minimize administrative effort.
