question:

question:

•Identify unknown traffic between the resources.
•Check the network connectivity between the virtual machines.
What should you use to perform each action? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. Identify unknown traffic between the resources : Virtual Network flow logs.
Virtual Network (VNet) flow logs provide detailed traffic insights at the Network Security Group (NSG) level.
They capture IP traffic flowing in and out of Azure resources, helping to:
Identify unexpected traffic between resources.
Detect suspicious activity.
Analyze security risks and network performance.

End-to-end connectivity monitoring between Azure VMs or other resources.
Continuous checking of latency, packet loss, and reachability.

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains the resources shown in the following table.
VNet1 and VNet2 are peered with each other. Subnet1 contains only VM1.
You need to configure Subnet11 to support 500 IP addresses. The solution must ensure that all traffic is routed between VNet1 and VNet2 and users can access App1.
Which three actions should you perform in sequence? To answer, move the appropriate actions from the list of actions to the answer area and arrange them in the correct order.
explanation:
1. Stop and deallocate VM1 A running VM is bound to a NIC configuration that can’t be modified in real time for some networking changes.
You must stop and deallocate a VM to:
Move its NIC to another subnet.
Change its private IP address allocation.
Modify subnet addressing.
What happens when you deallocate:
The VM’s compute resources are released, and its NIC configuration can then be changed safely.
2. Increase the size of Subnet11 If Subnet11 runs out of IP addresses, or you need to include a larger range, you must resize (increase) the subnet address range.
Prerequisites:
The VNet’s address space must have unused IP range contiguous to Subnet11.
You can only expand into free space that doesn’t overlap another subnet.
Important note:
This operation is online — you don’t have to delete or recreate the subnet if it’s an expansion (only contraction requires resource movement).
3. Sync the peerings between VNet1 and VNet2 When you modify a VNet’s address space (for example, you add a new subnet or extend an existing one), you must re-sync the peering to make sure both VNets recognize the updated address ranges.
Otherwise:
Connectivity may fail because one side still references the old network configuration.
How to do it:
Azure Portal → VNet → Peerings → click “Sync”

question:

You need to load balance network traffic across the virtual machines by using a cross-region load balancer solution.
Which type of load balancer should you deploy to each region, and which type of rule should you configure for the cross-region load balancer? To answer, select the appropriate options in the answer area.

question:
- You have an Azure subscription that contains the resources shown in the following table.
Policy1 has the following settings:
•Service: Microsoft Storage •Allowed Resources: storage1 Subnet1 has the following settings:
•Name: Subnet •Subnet address range: 10.0.0.0/24 •NAT gateway: None •Network security group: None •Route table: None •Service Endpoints oServices: Microsoft Storage oService endpoint policies: Policy1 •Subnet delegation oDelegate subnet to a service: None Subnet2 has the following settings:
•Name: Subnet2 •Subnet address range: 10.0.1.0/24 •NAT gateway: None •Network security group: None •Route table: None •Service Endpoints oServices: 0 selected •Subnet Delegation oDelegate subnet to a service: None For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
1. Devices on Subnet1 can access storage1 (Yes) The Configuration: Subnet1 has been granted access to the storage account via a Service Endpoint or a Virtual Network Rule.
Why it works: When you configure a storage account to allow traffic from a specific subnet, Azure adds that subnet’s ID to the storage account’s firewall. Any resource (like a VM) inside Subnet1 now has a "green light" to pass through the storage firewall.
2. Devices on Subnet2 can access storage1 (Yes) The Configuration: Similar to Subnet1, Subnet2 has also been explicitly added to the allowed networks list for storage1.
Why it works: Network security in Azure storage is additive. You can whitelist multiple subnets from the same VNet or different VNets. Since both subnets are marked "Yes," they are both part of the storage account's "Firewalls and virtual networks" allow-list.
3. Devices on Subnet1 can access storage2 (No) The Configuration: Subnet1 has not been added to the whitelist for storage2, or storage2 has its firewall set to "Disabled" or "Selected networks" without Subnet1 included.
Why it fails: By default, when you secure an Azure Storage account to specific networks, all other traffic is blocked. Even though Subnet1 can see storage1, it is treated as an external, untrusted source by storage2.

question:
- You have two Azure subscriptions named Sub1 and Sub2. Each subscription is linked to a separate Microsoft Entra tenant. Sub1 contains a virtual machine scale set that hosts an app named App1. Sub2 contains a virtual network named VNet1.
You need to connect VNet1 to App1 by using an Azure Private Link service.
Which four actions should you perform in sequence? To answer, move the appropriate actions from the list of actions to the answer area and arrange them in the correct order.
explanation:
Configure DB1 to run behind a Standard Azure load balancer.
Why: A Private Link service (custom) exposes your service through a front-end (the load balancer) that distributes traffic to the backend VMs/instances (your DB). Azure requires the Standard SKU load balancer for custom Private Link services. Make sure DB VM NICs (or VMSS) are in the LB backend pool, health probes and rules are configured for the DB port(s).
In Sub1, create a Private Link service to reference the load balancer.
Why: The provider (Sub1) creates the Private Link service resource and points it to the load balancer/backend.
This resource is what consumers will connect to; it exposes a service ID (resource ID) that consumers use to request connectivity.
In Sub2, create a private endpoint by using the Private Link service ID.
Why: The consumer (Sub2) creates a Private Endpoint resource in their VNet and specifies the provider’s Private Link service ID (or alias). This creates a NIC in the consumer VNet with a private IP and initiates a connection request to the provider’s service.
Approve the connection request.
Why: The provider must accept the incoming connection request (portal/CLI/PowerShell) unless auto-approval was configured. Once approved, the private endpoint is linked to the provider’s service and traffic flows privately over the Azure backbone to the load balancer → DB backend.

question:
- You have two Azure subscriptions named Sub1 and Sub2. Each subscription is linked to a separate Microsoft Entra tenant. Sub1 contains a virtual machine scale set that hosts an app named App1. Sub2 contains a virtual network named VNet1.

question:
Introductory Info Case Study - This is a case study. Case studies are not timed separately. You can use as much exam time as you would like to complete each case. However, there may be additional case studies and sections on this exam. You must manage your time to ensure that you are able to complete all questions included on this exam in the time provided.
To answer the questions included in a case study, you will need to reference information that is provided in the case study. Case studies might contain exhibits and other resources that provide more information about the scenario that is described in the case study. Each question is independent of the other questions in this case study.
At the end of this case study, a review screen will appear. This screen allows you to review your answers and to make changes before you move to the next section of the exam. After you begin a new section, you cannot return to this section.
To start the case study - To display the first question in this case study, click the Next button. Use the buttons in the left pane to explore the content of the case study before you answer the questions. Clicking these buttons displays information such as business requirements, existing environment, and problem statements. When you are ready to answer a question, click the Question button to return to the question.
Overview - Litware, Inc. is a financial company that has a main datacenter in Boston and 20 branch offices across the United States. Users have Android, iOS, and Windows 10 devices.
Existing Environment - Hybrid Environment - The on-premises network contains an Active Directory forest named litwareinc.com that syncs to an Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) tenant named litwareinc.com by using Azure AD Connect.
All offices connect to a virtual network named Vnet1 by using a Site-to-Site VPN connection.
Azure Environment - Litware has an Azure subscription named Sub1 that is linked to the litwareinc.com Azure AD tenant. Sub1 contains resources in the East US Azure region as shown in the following table.

explanation:

question:
You need to provide access to storage2. The solution must meet the PaaS networking requirements and the business requirements.
Which connectivity method should you use?
explanation:
The correct answer is A: a private endpoint. Here's why:
The scenario emphasizes PaaS networking requirements and business needs for secure access to storage2 (an Azure Storage account). Private endpoints directly address these requirements by providing private IP addresses within your VNet for accessing Azure services. This eliminates the need to expose the storage account publicly, enhancing security.
Service endpoints (option D) also provide secure access to Azure services, but they work by adding a route to the storage account's public IP address range, allowing traffic originating from the specified VNet. While secure, this approach doesn't completely isolate the service from the public internet like a private endpoint does.
Azure Firewall (option B) provides centralized network security and can filter traffic, but it primarily protects against outbound traffic and doesn't inherently establish private connectivity to PaaS services. It could be used in conjunction with a private endpoint but isn't the direct solution for private access.
Azure Front Door (option C) is a global, scalable entry point that uses the Microsoft global edge network to deliver fast, reliable, and secure access to web applications, APIs, and content. It's not primarily designed to provide private network connectivity to PaaS services.
Private endpoints directly map to the need for PaaS networking and secure private access to storage2. They provide the most secure option because all traffic between the VNet and the storage account remains within the Microsoft Azure backbone network. This is preferred for security and compliance and aligns well with the PaaS networking requirement. They enable you to lock down storage accounts from public internet access entirely, enhancing security posture.
Further Reading:
Microsoft Documentation on Private Endpoints: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private- endpoint-overview Microsoft Documentation on Service Endpoints: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual- network/service-endpoints-overview

You need to configure GW1 to meet the network security requirements for the P2S VPN users.
Which Tunnel type should you select in the Point-to-site configuration settings of GW1?
explanation:
The correct answer is D. OpenVPN (SSL). Here's why:
The question focuses on network security requirements for Point-to-Site (P2S) VPN users. OpenVPN (SSL) offers robust security features essential for this scenario.
SSL/TLS Encryption: OpenVPN leverages the well-established SSL/TLS protocol suite for encryption. This encryption secures the data transmitted between the client and the VPN gateway (GW1), preventing eavesdropping and unauthorized access.
Flexibility and Customization: OpenVPN is highly configurable, allowing administrators to fine-tune security parameters like cipher suites, key lengths, and authentication methods to meet specific security needs.
Firewall Traversal: OpenVPN can operate over UDP or TCP ports, making it easier to traverse restrictive firewalls and Network Address Translation (NAT) devices, which is critical for ensuring reliable connectivity for P2S VPN users connecting from various locations.
Strong Authentication: OpenVPN supports various authentication methods, including certificates and usernames/passwords, enabling multi-factor authentication for enhanced security.
While IKEv2 offers good performance and security, it primarily focuses on site-to-site VPNs. SSTP (SSL), while also using SSL, is known to have some potential security vulnerabilities compared to OpenVPN and might not be as widely supported across different client operating systems. The combination options (A and C) include IKEv2, which isn't the primary choice for enhanced security focused P2S VPN scenarios and also adds unnecessary protocol complexity for this specific use case.
Therefore, OpenVPN (SSL) provides the best balance of security, flexibility, and client compatibility for securing P2S VPN connections.
Authoritative Links:
About Point-to-site VPN: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway-about-point-to- site-vpn Configure OpenVPN for P2S VPN: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway- howto-openvpn

question: What should you implement to meet the virtual network requirements for the virtual machines that connect to Vnet4 and Vnet5?
explanation:
The correct answer is E, virtual network peering. Let's justify this choice:
Virtual network peering establishes a direct connection between two virtual networks, enabling resources in each network to communicate with each other as if they were in the same network. This satisfies the requirement for VMs connected to Vnet4 and Vnet5 to communicate. Virtual network peering supports both intra-region and inter-region connections, and traffic between the virtual networks stays within the Microsoft Azure backbone network, ensuring secure and low-latency communication.
Private endpoints (A) securely connect to an Azure service via a private IP address from your virtual network.
They are for connecting to services outside the VNet, not for connecting two VNets. Routing tables (B) control the routing of traffic within a virtual network, but they don't create the actual connection between networks.
Service endpoints (C) provide secure and direct connectivity to Azure services from within your virtual network. They are similar to private endpoints, allowing access to services like Azure Storage or Azure SQL Database, but not for inter-VNet communication. Private link service (D) enables you to expose your own service behind a standard Azure load balancer and make it available to others privately. This is useful for service providers to offer services privately to their customers.
Therefore, virtual network peering is the most suitable solution for directly connecting Vnet4 and Vnet5 to facilitate communication between the virtual machines deployed in these networks. Peering offers a direct, secure, and performant connection.
For more information, refer to:
Azure virtual network peering: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network- peering-overview

question:
You need to connect Vnet2 and Vnet3. The solution must meet the virtual networking requirements and the business requirements.
Which two actions should you include in the solution? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
options: On the peering from Vnet1, select Allow gateway transit.
On the peerings from Vnet2 and Vnet3, select Use remote gateways.
On the peerings from Vnet2 and Vnet3, select Allow gateway transit.
On the peering from Vnet1, select Use remote gateways.
On the peering from Vnet1, select Allow forwarded traffic.
explanation:
The correct answer is A and B. Here's why:
Virtual Network Peering with Gateway Transit: The core problem is to connect Vnet2 and Vnet3 while leveraging the existing VPN gateway in Vnet1. This requires configuring Vnet Peering with gateway transit.
Gateway transit enables Vnet2 and Vnet3 to use the VPN gateway located in Vnet1.
Allow Gateway Transit on Vnet1's Peering: To enable Vnet1 to share its gateway, you need to allow gateway transit on the peering from Vnet1 to each of the other VNets (Vnet2 and Vnet3). This means that from the Vnet1 side of the peering, it is allowing the other side to use its gateway.
Use Remote Gateways on Vnet2 and Vnet3's Peerings: Vnet2 and Vnet3 need to specify that they should use the remote gateways. This setting is applied on each of the peerings from Vnet2 to Vnet1 and from Vnet3 to Vnet1. In essence, Vnet2 and Vnet3 are configured to look for a gateway in the peered network (Vnet1).
Why C is incorrect: Selecting "Allow gateway transit" on the peerings from Vnet2 and Vnet3 to Vnet1 is incorrect. Vnet2 and Vnet3 are not providing the gateway; they are using a gateway in a remote network. The "Allow gateway transit" setting is for the network that owns the gateway (Vnet1).
Why D and E are incorrect: Option D, "Use remote gateways" on Vnet1's peering, is backward in logic. Vnet1 is the one providing the gateway. Option E, "Allow forwarded traffic", is a more general setting related to traffic flow within and between peered VNets and isn't specific to gateway transit.
In summary: Enabling gateway transit involves two complementary actions: the gateway-owning VNet (Vnet1) must allow gateway transit, and the gateway-using VNets (Vnet2 and Vnet3) must be configured to use remote gateways.
Authoritative Links:
About VPN gateway transit for virtual network peering: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn- gateway/vpn-gateway-peering-gateway-transit Create a virtual network peering using the Azure portal: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual- network/tutorial-connect-virtual-networks-portal - Note this article contains examples of how to configure peering, including the gateway transit option.

question: What should you implement to meet the virtual network requirements for the virtual machines that connect to Vnet4 and Vnet5?
explanation:
The correct answer is E (virtual network peering) because it directly addresses the requirement to connect virtual machines in Vnet4 and Vnet5. Virtual network peering enables you to seamlessly connect Azure virtual networks, allowing resources in each network to communicate with each other as if they were in the same network. This includes direct IP connectivity between virtual machines, as well as name resolution through a shared DNS server.
Private endpoints (A) provide private connectivity to Azure services, not between virtual networks. Routing tables (B) control the routing of traffic within a virtual network or subnet, but don't establish connectivity between networks. Service endpoints (C) provide secure and direct connectivity to Azure services from within your virtual network but don't facilitate general inter-VNet communication. Private link service (D) allows you to expose your own service deployed behind a standard load balancer privately in your customer's VNet.
Virtual network peering establishes a direct, private connection between the two virtual networks, fulfilling the need for virtual machines in Vnet4 and Vnet5 to communicate. Without peering, virtual machines in different virtual networks would generally not be able to directly communicate using private IP addresses.
Peering removes the need for more complex and potentially costly solutions like VPN gateways for this simple connectivity scenario. It's the most straightforward and efficient method for meeting the stated requirements.
Authoritative Links:
Azure Virtual Network Peering: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network- peering-overview

question:
You need to configure the default route on Vnet2 and Vnet3. The solution must meet the virtual networking requirements.
What should you use to configure the default route?
options: a user-defined route assigned to GatewaySubnet in Vnet1
a user-defined route assigned to GatewaySubnet in Vnet2 and Vnet3
explanation:
The correct answer is B. BGP route exchange.
Here's why:
The scenario requires configuring the default route on VNet2 and VNet3 to route traffic to VNet1's virtual network gateway. BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the standard routing protocol used to exchange routing information between autonomous systems (AS). In Azure, it enables the virtual network gateway to learn the routes of the other virtual networks it connects to and propagate the default route (0.0.0.0/0) to them.
Here's a breakdown of why the other options are incorrect and why BGP is optimal:
1. A. Route Filters: Route filters are used to filter which routes are advertised from a virtual network gateway, not to set a default route within a virtual network. They are used for selective route advertisement, not overall routing configuration.
2. C. a user-defined route assigned to GatewaySubnet in Vnet1: User-defined routes (UDRs) on the GatewaySubnet of VNet1 would influence how traffic leaving the gateway is routed, not how traffic within VNet2 and VNet3 finds its way to the gateway. This controls outbound, not inbound, routing perspective of VNet1's gateway.
3. D. a user-defined route assigned to GatewaySubnet in Vnet2 and Vnet3: While you can use UDRs to direct traffic within VNet2 and VNet3 to VNet1's gateway, this approach is less dynamic and scalable compared to BGP. Any change in the gateway's IP address or network configuration would require manual updates to the UDRs in both VNets. BGP automatically handles route propagation and updates.
BGP, when enabled on the Azure virtual network gateway, automates the route propagation. The gateway learns the routes and redistributes this information to the connected virtual networks. When VNet2 and VNet3 receive the default route from VNet1's gateway via BGP, they know to forward any traffic destined for outside their respective address spaces to that gateway. This simplifies routing configuration and is the recommended approach for complex, dynamic network topologies. This is especially important when the IP addresses and routes of underlying services and systems may change over time. The dynamic nature of BGP ensures the routing remains current and correct.
Here are some authoritative links for further research:
1. About VPN Gateway BGP Support: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn- gateway-bgp 2. Configure BGP: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway-howto-bgp 3. User-defined routes and IP forwarding: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual- network/virtual-networks-udr-overview
